case_name: Tebufenozide removal from well water for a small drinking water system
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for tebufenozide-contaminated well water intended for drinking water use under low-energy, small-system constraints.

input_data.scenario_background: A small rural drinking water system relies on a shallow production well located near orchards and mixed agricultural land. Routine screening detected tebufenozide at a level of concern, while the source water otherwise has relatively stable groundwater characteristics. The utility has limited staff, prefers simple and robust treatment, and wants to avoid energy-intensive membrane systems unless clearly necessary.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve reliable reduction of tebufenozide to a level protective for drinking water use while maintaining overall potable water quality.
secondary_goals: keep operation and monitoring requirements manageable
address moderate natural organic matter that may affect adsorption performance

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: limited residuals handling capacity; periodic media changeout is feasible but complex hazardous liquid waste handling is undesirable
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: stable unattended operation with straightforward operator oversight is required

core_unit_functions.prefiltration: remove incidental particulates and protect downstream adsorptive media
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_adsorption: adsorb tebufenozide and other trace hydrophobic organic compounds from groundwater
core_unit_functions.post_filtration: retain carbon fines and polish finished water
core_unit_functions.disinfection: provide microbiological protection for drinking water distribution

applicability_rationale: tebufenozide is an organic micropollutant for which activated carbon is a well-established barrier technology in drinking water treatment
groundwater turbidity is low enough that a simple prefiltration plus GAC configuration is practical for a small system
moderate DOC indicates possible adsorption competition, but not at a level that makes GAC unsuitable; it mainly supports the need for media monitoring and replacement planning
the process is low energy, modular, and widely compatible with small drinking water facilities
potential_risks: DOC competition can shorten carbon bed life and require more frequent media changeout
breakthrough may occur if EBCT and monitoring are insufficient
spent carbon handling and replacement logistics must be planned

core_unit_functions.prefiltration: stabilize solids loading before adsorption and solids separation
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon_contact: provide adsorption of tebufenozide during batch or continuous contact
core_unit_functions.cartridge_or_multimedia_filtration: remove PAC solids and adsorbed contaminant-bearing particles
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure microbiological safety of distributed water

applicability_rationale: activated carbon remains an appropriate removal mechanism for hydrophobic pesticide residues
PAC can be a feasible alternative when small systems want lower initial capital cost than fixed-bed GAC
the treatment train remains relatively low energy and can be added to existing filtration assets if available
potential_risks: chemical feed control and contact time management are more operator-dependent than fixed-bed GAC
PAC slurry handling and solids disposal increase routine operational burden
performance can vary more under fluctuating contaminant loading

core_unit_functions.air_stripping_or_aeration_not_primary: provide basic water conditioning but not primary tebufenozide removal
core_unit_functions.advanced_oxidation: oxidize tebufenozide and transform recalcitrant organic molecules
core_unit_functions.biologically_active_or_gac_filtration: remove oxidation byproducts and polish remaining organics

applicability_rationale: advanced oxidation can address pesticides and other trace organic contaminants where adsorption alone is insufficient or polishing is required
pairing oxidation with downstream carbon filtration is a recognized route for micropollutant control
this approach is technically strong but less aligned with the stated preference for simple, low-energy operation
potential_risks: higher energy use and more complex control than adsorption-only systems
oxidation byproduct formation and quench requirements may need careful management
advanced oxidation may be unnecessarily complex for a single primary contaminant in otherwise stable well water

core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_reverse_osmosis: provide broad-spectrum rejection of dissolved organic contaminants and salts
core_unit_functions.stabilization: restore appropriate finished water chemistry after membrane treatment
core_unit_functions.disinfection: protect the drinking water distribution system

applicability_rationale: pressure membrane treatment can provide a strong barrier to many organic micropollutants including pesticide residues
it may be considered if future source water screening indicates multiple dissolved contaminants rather than a single dominant pesticide issue
for the stated case, it is technically capable but not the most proportionate response
potential_risks: high energy demand conflicts with the low-energy constraint
concentrate management is difficult for a small rural system
membrane scaling, cleaning, and stabilization needs add operational complexity

core_unit_functions.conventional_coagulation: destabilize colloids and some associated natural organic matter

applicability_rationale: conventional clarification is proven for particulate and NOM control in many drinking water applications
it may provide some indirect benefit if a fraction of tebufenozide is associated with organic matter, but it is not a primary removal barrier for dissolved pesticide contamination in groundwater
the train is less targeted than activated carbon for this specific contaminant
potential_risks: likely inadequate standalone removal for dissolved tebufenozide
chemical feed, sludge generation, and solids handling are burdensome for a small well-water system
may add process complexity without reliably meeting the primary contaminant objective

preferred_rank_pattern: activated_carbon_based_processes should rank at the top for tebufenozide in well water
high-pressure_membrane_options should not rank first under low_energy_constraint and small_system conditions
conventional_coagulation_sedimentation_should rank below targeted adsorption for a dissolved pesticide case

must_reflect: low energy preference should favor passive or low-power adsorptive treatment
small system operation should favor simple modular treatment and manageable monitoring
recommendations should avoid unnecessarily complex residuals or concentrate management

must_contain: why activated carbon is appropriate for pesticide-like organic micropollutants
how DOC or natural organic matter can reduce adsorption capacity through competition
why membrane and advanced oxidation options rank lower despite technical capability

should_mention: carbon breakthrough and media replacement frequency
matrix effects from DOC on adsorption performance
residuals or concentrate handling implications for small systems
